\section{Introduction}

% Introduction to actor models and their communication mechanism.
Actor models provide a computational framework for concurrent systems through
asynchronous message-passing mechanisms. In these systems, actors are
independent computational units that encapsulate the execution environment of
their processing logic. Communication between actors occurs solely through
asynchronous message passing, where messages are stored in the recipient's
mailbox ---a dedicated piece of storage that preserves message order while
remaining separate from the actor's core logic.

% Challenges in actor system message flow management
Actor systems face challenges managing message flow under high volumes. While
message delivery is often assumed reliable, practical implementations must
handle message loss/delays from processing bottlenecks. As in theory, conventional
implementations couple mailboxes with actors, causing overflow risks without
flow control. Though \emph{backpressure} techniques may help regulate message
rates, these control mechanisms may also fail under heavy load, particularly
with high-volume producers serving multiple consumers.

% Proposes a solution by decoupling mailboxes from processing actors.
To address these challenges, here we propose decoupling mailboxes from their
processing actors by implementing each mailbox as a separate actor entity.
In this design, communication occurs through message passing to dedicated
mailbox actors, with processing actors pulling messages only when ready to
handle them. We give an explicit specification of the message-passing mechanism,
so the mailbox actors remain opaque to both senders and receivers.

% Outlines the benefits of the proposed architectural separation.
This architectural separation of mailboxes from processing actors provides
several key benefits. First, it cleanly separates message management from
processing logic, allowing mailbox actors to specialize in queue management
while processing actors focus on their core functionality. Second, it enables
flexible message scheduling—different mailbox implementations can support
priority queues, batch processing, or custom dispatch strategies without affecting
processing actors. Third, mailbox actors become modular components that can be
modified or replaced independently.

% Discusses the trade-offs and potential drawbacks of the design.
This design may come with some trade-offs. The primary drawback we foresee is
the increased system complexity due to additional actors and message passing
overhead. That is, higher resource usage from running more actors, and potential
latency increases when mailbox actors run on different nodes of the system than
their processing actors. However, with careful design, these costs can be
outweighed by the benefits of a more flexible and scalable system.

% Mentions the potential for future work in extending the design.
The design also naturally extends to support multiple mailbox actors per
processing actor if needed, with minimal additional complexity due to the
simplicity of mailbox actors and their well-defined interfaces. However, we
leave this as future work, as it is not a requirement for the core design.

% How this paper is organized and what is the contribution.

\paragraph{Contributions} We propose a new architectural
separation between processing actors and their mailboxes in \Cref{sec:model},
which enables improved flexibility and scalability for message management. Second,
we formulate operational semantics in \Cref{sec:semantics} for both the decoupled
architecture and its explicit message-passing mechanism, making the system's
communication patterns formally explicit. This description provides sufficient
grounding to implement a library on top of a compatible system/platform such
as Erlang, and formalise and prove key system properties such as liveness
guarantees and correctness under concurrent message processing. 

\section{Model}\label{sec:model}

The model used in this paper is an extended version of the actor model based on
\emph{engines}~\cite{engine-art}. So this model mainly consists of engines,
which are our actor equivalent notion. Engines load more information than standard
actors. For example, an engine comprises a configuration, an execution context,
and a processing logic referred to as \emph{behaviour}. The configuration
includes, for example, the engine's name, network location, node, and an
optional reference to the parent engine. The execution context includes an
engine-specific local state and address book of other engines to communicate
with, including the engine's own address. The behaviour includes a set of
\emph{guarded actions} that specify how the engine reacts to incoming messages.
An engine only reacts to a message if its behaviour contains a guarded action
that satisfies a number of pre-conditions, these are called \emph{guards}. The
actual processing logic is determined by the \emph{actions}, compounds of atomic
effects such as sending a message or terminating an engine. 

This version of the actor model provides more expressive power than the standard
actor model, and should not be considered a limitation, but rather an extension
that allows a more fine-grained control over engine-level properties and
specifications.

Engines interact within a system governed by a given message-passing mechanism
that enforces policies for message delivery, ordering, loss, and flow control.
For example, this mechanism enforces that only engines send messages to other
engines, and no other forms of communication are allowed.

\subsection{Notation}

For the sake of clarity, we recall notation and conventions used in this paper.
We use type-theoretic notation for types and terms, meaning that a type is of
type $\mathsf{Type}$ where $\mathsf{Type}$ is the type of types. A term $x$ of
type $A$ is denoted as $x : A$. Capital letters or capitalized words are used for
types, and lowercase letters or lowercase words are used for terms. 

$\N$ denotes the type of natural numbers. The type of addresses is
$\mathsf{Addr}$ and the type of engine names is $\mathsf{Name}$, both of which
are type synonyms for $\N$, for simplicity.

Types include sets, and we work with the following set-theoretic notation: for
sets $A$ and $B$: $A \subseteq B$ denotes set inclusion, meaning every element
of $A$ belongs to $B$; $\varnothing$ denotes the empty set; $A \times B$ denotes
the Product type of $A$ and $B$, where $\langle a,b \rangle$ denotes an ordered
pair where $a : A$ and $b : B$. A \emph{tuple} $\langle a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n
\rangle$ is a generalisation of an ordered pair to an ordered $n$-tuple, where
$n \in \N$, of type $A_1 \times \cdots \times A_n$, and $a_i$ is of type $A_i$
for all $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. The projections are $\pi_1(a) :\equiv a_1$ and
$\pi_2(a) :\equiv a_2$. Additionally, $A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \cdots \cup A_n$ denotes
sum type of $A_1, \dots, A_n$, so $\mathsf{inj_1}(a_1)$ and $\mathsf{inj_2}(a_2)$
are injections into $A_1 \cup A_2$ from $A_1$ and $A_2$ respectively.


\subsubsection{Entities}

\begin{definition}\label{def:processing-engine}
A processing engine is defined by a tuple $\langle b,\, m,\, r,\, \mathsf{cfg},\, \mathsf{env} \rangle$ where:
\begin{itemize}
\item $b$ is the engine behaviour (a set of guarded actions)
\item $m$ is the address of the dedicated mailbox engine
\item $r$ is the status flag ($\mathsf{ready}$, $\mathsf{busy}$, $\mathsf{terminated}$)
\item $\mathsf{cfg}$ is the engine configuration including name, network location, node, and optional parent
\item $\mathsf{env}$ is the execution context including local state $\sigma$ and address book
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\label{def:mailbox-engine}
A mailbox engine is a specialized processing engine that acts as a message buffer between engines. Its primary responsibilities are:

\begin{enumerate}
\item Receiving and storing messages sent to its associated processing engine
\item Forwarding stored messages to the processing engine when it is ready to receive them
\item Preventing message loss and managing load on the processing engine
\end{enumerate}

There are only two operations allowed on a mailbox engine:

\begin{itemize}
\item $\mathsf{append}$: Append a message to the mailbox
\item $\mathsf{pull}$: Pull a message from the mailbox
\end{itemize}

The mailbox engine's state can be implemented using various data structures depending on the desired message handling semantics, for example:

\begin{itemize}
\item FIFO queues for standard first-in-first-out processing
\item Priority queues for message prioritization
\end{itemize}

For the processing engine, the mailbox engine is an opaque object. The
processing engine only reads messages, one by one, from the mailbox engine when
it is ready to receive them.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\label{def:messages}
$\mathsf{Msg}$ refers to the type of message payloads/interfaces, which is a disjoint union of all engine-specific message interfaces. For example, a mailbox engine accepts messages like $\mathsf{append}$ and $\mathsf{pull}$, while a processing engine accepts domain-specific messages.

Messages are formulated as triples $\langle s,\, t,\, v \rangle$ where:
\begin{itemize}
\item $s$ is the sender engine's address
\item $t$ is the target engine's address 
\item $v$ is the payload of type $\mathsf{Msg}$
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\label{def:guards}
A guard is a function $g$ of type $\mathsf{Msg} \times \mathsf{Env} \times \mathsf{Cfg} \to \mathsf{R}$ where:
\begin{itemize}
\item $\mathsf{Msg}$ is the type of messages
\item $\mathsf{Env}$ is the type of engine environments
\item $\mathsf{Cfg}$ is the type of engine configurations
\item $\mathsf{R}$ is the type of guard results. In case it is nothing, it is represented as $\bot$, meaning that the guard does not match
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}\label{def:effects}
Effects $\mathcal{E}$ are defined by the grammar:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:effects}
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{E} &::= \mathsf{send}(\mathsf{Addr}, \mathsf{Msg}) & &\text{\% Message dispatch} \\
&\mid \mathsf{update}(\mathsf{Env} \to \mathsf{Env}) & &\text{\% Environment modification} \\
&\mid \mathsf{spawn}(\mathsf{Cfg}, \mathsf{Env}) & &\text{\% Engine creation} \\
&\mid \mathsf{chain}(\mathcal{E}, \mathcal{E}) & &\text{\% Sequential composition} \\
&\mid \mathsf{terminate} & &\text{\% Engine termination} \\
&\mid \mathsf{nop} & &\text{\% No operation}
\end{aligned}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Global System Configuration]\label{def:global-config}
We need a system to track the in-transit messages. A pair $\kappa :\equiv (\alpha, \mu)$ with:

\begin{itemize}
\item $\alpha$: A mapping from engine addresses to the actual engine instance. If the engine is a mailbox engine, as an abuse of notation, we treat it as its local state, that is, the data structure storing the messages.
\item $\mu$: A (multi)set of in-transit messages.
\end{itemize}

With the system defined, there are several associated functions:

\begin{itemize}
\item $\mathsf{mailboxOf}$: The address of the mailbox engine for engine $e$.

\[
\mathsf{mailboxOf}(e) :\equiv \begin{cases}
  m_e & \text{if } e\equiv\langle b,\, m_e,\, r,\, \mathsf{cfg},\, \mathsf{env} \rangle \text{ is a processing engine} \\
  \bot & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\]

\item $\mathsf{freshId}$: A function that returns a fresh engine address
\item $\mathsf{freshName}$: A function that returns a fresh engine name
\item $\execEffect(E, e, (\alpha, \mu))$: A function that executes/runs an effect $E$ for engine $e$ and returns an updated configuration and a new engine status. The engine status could be $\mathsf{ready}$, $\mathsf{busy}$, or $\mathsf{terminated}$. The latter is achieved when the effect is $\mathsf{terminate}$, $\mathsf{ready}$ when everything goes well, and $\mathsf{busy}$ is not expected to happen.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}

\section{Operational Semantics}\label{sec:semantics}

We formalise the reduction rules for our operational semantics through the relation $\kappa_n \to \kappa_{n+1}$, where $\kappa_n \equiv (\alpha_n, \mu_n)$ represents the system state. Mailbox engines are modeled as sets for notational convenience, with message operations handled through message passing.

\subsection{System Transition Rules}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Send}]\label{rule:send}
Processing engine $e$ sends message $v$ to target $e_{\mathsf{target}}$:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:send}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  m_{\mathsf{target}} \equiv \mathsf{mailboxOf}(e_{\mathsf{target}}) \quad
  v : \mathsf{Msg}
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha, \mu \cup \{ \langle e, m_{\mathsf{target}}, v \rangle \})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Append}]\label{rule:append}
Mailbox engine $e$ enqueues message $v$:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:append}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv q \quad
  \langle s, e, v \rangle \in \mu
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha[e \mapsto q \cup \{v\}], \mu \setminus \{\langle s, e, v \rangle\})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Pull}]\label{rule:pull}
Processing engine retrieves message from mailbox:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:pull}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  \alpha(m_e) \equiv \{v\} \cup q
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha', \mu)
}
\end{equation}
where $\alpha' \equiv \alpha[e \mapsto \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle] \cup \{ m_e \mapsto q \}$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Clean}]\label{rule:clean}
Remove terminated engine:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:clean}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{terminated}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha \setminus \{e\}, \mu)
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{Process}]\label{rule:process}
Engine processes message:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:process}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, \mathsf{busy}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  b, \mathsf{env} \vdash v \Downarrow E \quad
  \execEffect(E, e, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha', \mu', r')
}{
  (\alpha, \mu) \to (\alpha', \mu')
}
\end{equation}
where $r' \equiv \mathsf{ready}$ if $r' \neq \mathsf{terminated}$.
\end{definition}

\subsection{Effect Execution Rules}

The function $\execEffect(E, a, (\alpha, \mu))$ is defined recursively:

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-sendMsg}]\label{rule:e-send}
\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-send}
\frac{
  \mathsf{msg} \equiv \langle e_{\mathsf{sender}}, e_{\mathsf{target}}, v \rangle \quad
  m_{\mathsf{target}} \equiv \mathsf{mailboxOf}(e_{\mathsf{target}})
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{sendMsg}(\mathsf{msg}), a, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv ((\alpha, \mu \cup \{ \mathsf{msg} \}), \mathsf{ready})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-updateEnv}]\label{rule:e-update}
\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-update}
\frac{
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \quad
  \mathsf{env}^{*} : \mathsf{ofType}(\mathsf{env})
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{updateEnv}(\mathsf{env}^{*}), e, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha[e \mapsto \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env}^{*} \rangle], \mathsf{ready})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-spawnEngine}]\label{rule:e-spawn}
Create new engine:
\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-spawn}
\frac{
  n \equiv \mathsf{freshId}(\alpha, \mu) \quad
  m \equiv \mathsf{freshId}(\alpha, \mu) \quad
  \alpha(e) \equiv \langle b, m_e, r, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{spawnEngine}(e), a, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha \cup \{ n \mapsto \langle b, m, \mathsf{ready}, \mathsf{cfg}, \mathsf{env} \rangle \}, \mathsf{ready})
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[\textsf{E-chain}]\label{rule:e-chain}
Sequential composition:

\begin{equation}\label{eq:e-chain}
\frac{
  \execEffect(e_1, a, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha_1, \mu_1, s_1) \quad
  \execEffect(\mathsf{chain}(e_2; \dots; e_n), a, (\alpha_1, \mu_1)) \equiv (\alpha', \mu', s')
}{
  \execEffect(\mathsf{chain}(e_1; \dots; e_n), e, (\alpha, \mu)) \equiv (\alpha', \mu', s')
}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

The complete operational semantics comprises three components:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Configuration transitions via Rules \textsf{Send}--\textsf{Process}
\item Effect execution through Rules \textsf{E-sendMsg}--\textsf{E-chain}
\item Type safety through Definition~\ref{def:effects}
\end{enumerate}

This system formalises message passing (via Rules~\ref{rule:send}--\ref{rule:append}), message processing (Rule~\ref{rule:process}), and effect-driven state changes as defined in Section~\ref{sec:semantics}.

\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related}

Prior work has explored decoupling mailboxes from actors, primarily through
techniques like work-queues where actors pull messages when ready to process
them. Libraries like WeChaty in JavaScript have implemented similar mailbox
separation patterns. However, our work is novel in treating mailboxes as
first-class primitives within the actor model itself.
